worth_it_explanation: An Advantage+ Sales Campaign shares delivery decisions across creative, audience, placements, and budget, so a structural mistake here moves real spend. When an ASC and a manual sales campaign chase the same products with no separation, they bid against each other and Meta's auction costs rise. When the existing customer budget cap is left at its default, acquisition budget drifts toward people who would have bought anyway. These checks read in Ads Manager and Business Settings without any change to the account, and the fixes are configuration steps rather than rebuilds.
intro: An Advantage+ Sales Campaign (ASC) hands most of the delivery decisions to Meta's automation, so the levers that remain, how it sits next to your other campaigns, how the budget splits between new and existing customers, and whether the conversion signal feeding it is healthy, carry more weight than they would in a manual campaign. These checks confirm that an ASC is not overlapping a manual sales campaign on the same catalog, that the existing customer budget cap reflects the goal of the campaign, and that the conversion data the campaign optimizes toward is solid enough for automated delivery to learn from.

audit_checks.title: An Advantage+ Sales Campaign and a manual sales campaign run against the same catalog with no separation
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When two sales campaigns chase the same products and audiences, they enter the same auctions and bid against each other, which raises the price Meta charges to reach those people. The automated campaign also learns from a budget the manual campaign is partly absorbing, so neither reaches the volume its budget implies.

educate.base:
An Advantage+ Sales Campaign (ASC) optimizes delivery across creative, audience, placements, and budget toward purchases, drawing from a connected catalog or pixel and Conversions API (CAPI) signal. A manual sales campaign optimizes toward the same purchase event but with the targeting, placements, and budget you set yourself.

When both run against the same products at the same time, they can enter the same auctions for the same people. Meta does not net out a self-competing bid for you. Each campaign bids as if the other did not exist, so the price to reach a shared audience climbs, an effect Meta describes as auction overlap. The automated campaign is the one that suffers most because its delivery model assumes it is seeing the full demand for those products rather than a slice the manual campaign is also buying.

Meta provides an auction overlap diagnostic and a recommendation to consolidate overlapping campaigns, both of which surface this case. Consolidating spend into fewer campaigns also gives the automated delivery more conversions to learn from, which it needs to stabilize.

A few signs point to an overlap worth resolving:
  • An ASC and a manual sales campaign target the same catalog or product set during the same dates
  • Ads Manager shows an auction overlap warning on one or both campaigns
  • Reach or frequency on the manual campaign moved sharply when the ASC launched
You may want to decide which campaign owns the catalog and pause or re-scope the other, rather than running both and paying the overlap.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the overlap usually traces to a manual catalog or sales campaign that predates the Advantage+ Sales Campaign and was never paused when the automated campaign launched. You may want to confirm whether the manual campaign still earns its budget on its own, or whether its products belong inside the automated campaign where the delivery model can see the full demand.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, a true catalog sales campaign is less common, so an apparent overlap is often an Advantage+ Sales Campaign sitting next to a manual conversion campaign optimizing toward the same lead event. You may want to confirm the two are optimizing toward different events or audiences before treating them as competing because a lead campaign and a sales campaign can coexist when their goals differ.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether an Advantage+ Sales Campaign and a manual sales campaign are targeting the same products or audiences at the same time, and check Ads Manager for an auction overlap warning.

meta_ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: Open Ads Manager and filter the campaign list to active sales campaigns. Note which are Advantage+ Sales Campaigns and which are manual.
For each pair that could overlap, compare the catalog or product set, the audience, and the active dates. A shared catalog running on overlapping dates is the case to flag.
Look for an auction overlap warning or a consolidation recommendation on the campaign. Open it and read which campaigns Meta names as overlapping.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each active sales campaign, its type (Advantage+ Sales Campaign or manual), the catalog or product set it targets, and whether Ads Manager flags an auction overlap. Two campaigns of different types targeting the same catalog on overlapping dates, especially with an overlap warning present, is a fail recorded with both campaign names. Sales campaigns that target distinct catalogs or audiences, or that do not overlap in time, pass.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active sales campaign's type, catalog, and any auction overlap warning above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs an Advantage+ Sales Campaign and a manual sales campaign against the same catalog at the same time. Two sales campaigns chasing the same products enter the same auctions and bid against each other, which raises the cost to reach those buyers, and the automated campaign learns from a budget the manual campaign is partly absorbing. {{voice_recommend}} deciding which campaign owns the catalog and either pausing the other or re-scoping it to a distinct product set or audience. Consolidating the spend also gives the automated delivery more conversions to learn from. After the change, watch Ads Manager for the auction overlap warning to clear and compare cost per purchase before and after.

audit_checks.title: The existing customer budget cap is left at its default on an acquisition-focused Advantage+ Sales Campaign
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without a cap, an Advantage+ Sales Campaign is free to spend the full budget on people who already buy from the brand, so reported return looks strong while genuine new-customer acquisition stays flat. A campaign meant to grow the customer base ends up paying to reach buyers who would have converted anyway.

educate.base:
An Advantage+ Sales Campaign (ASC) can spend across both new and existing customers. The existing customer budget cap is the control that limits how much of the budget reaches people Meta recognizes as current customers, leaving the rest for new-customer acquisition. The cap depends on a defined existing-customer audience, set in the campaign's reporting controls from a Customer list custom audience or a comparable source.

When the cap is left at its default, the campaign can allocate as much as the auction favors toward existing customers. Reaching current buyers is cheaper and converts at a higher rate, so an unconstrained ASC drifts in that direction. The reported return on ad spend (ROAS) looks healthy because retargeting your own customers is efficient, but the campaign is no longer doing the acquisition work it was set up for.

The cap is a strategic choice rather than a universal setting. A campaign built to grow the customer base wants a low cap or zero spend on existing customers. A campaign built to maximize total sales across both groups may want a higher cap or none at all. The audit question is whether the cap reflects the stated goal of the campaign, not whether a cap exists.

A few signs suggest the cap deserves a second look:
  • The campaign's goal is new-customer growth, but the existing customer budget cap is at its default with no limit applied
  • No existing-customer audience is defined, so the cap has nothing to act on
  • A large share of attributed purchases come from returning customers on a campaign meant to acquire
You may want to confirm the campaign's goal with the account owner, then check whether the cap and the existing-customer audience together match that goal.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store with repeat purchasers, the pull toward existing customers is strongest because a known buyer is the cheapest conversion in the auction. You may want to confirm the existing-customer audience is built from a current Customer list custom audience or order data so the cap acts on an accurate list, and that the cap reflects whether this campaign is for acquisition or for total sales.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, the equivalent question is whether the campaign should be reaching people already in the customer relationship management (CRM) system as closed customers. You may want to confirm an existing-customer or closed-won audience is defined as an exclusion or a capped segment so an acquisition campaign is not paying to re-reach accounts that already converted.

investigate.intro: Confirm the campaign's goal, then check whether the existing customer budget cap and the defined existing-customer audience match that goal.

meta_ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: Open the Advantage+ Sales Campaign in Ads Manager and find the existing customer settings. Note whether an existing-customer audience is defined and what budget cap, if any, is applied to it.
Open the campaign's breakdown by new versus returning customers, where available, and note how attributed purchases split between the two groups.
Compare the cap and the split against the stated goal of the campaign. A campaign meant to acquire, with no cap and a high share of returning-customer purchases, is the case to flag.

meta_business_settings.label: Meta Business Settings
steps: Open Business Settings and review the Customer list custom audience used as the existing-customer source.
Check when the list was last uploaded or refreshed. A stale list misclassifies recent buyers as new, which weakens the cap even when it is set.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the campaign's stated goal, whether an existing-customer audience is defined, the existing customer budget cap value, and the new-versus-returning purchase split where available. An acquisition campaign with no cap, no defined existing-customer audience, or a high returning-customer share is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the goal it was meant to serve. A campaign whose cap and audience match its goal passes.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the campaign goal, the existing customer budget cap, and the new-versus-returning split above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Advantage+ Sales Campaign is set up for new-customer growth but the existing customer budget cap is left at its default, so the campaign is free to spend the budget on people who already buy from the brand. Reaching existing customers is cheaper and converts well, which makes the reported return look strong while genuine acquisition stays flat. {{voice_recommend}} confirming the campaign's goal, defining an accurate existing-customer audience from a current customer list, and setting the cap to match that goal, low or zero for acquisition and higher for total-sales campaigns. After the change, watch the new-versus-returning purchase split to confirm the budget is reaching the intended group.

audit_checks.title: An Advantage+ Sales Campaign is running on a thin or unreliable conversion signal
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Automated delivery learns from the purchase signal it receives. When that signal is sparse, duplicated, or missing the Conversions API, the campaign optimizes toward incomplete or noisy data, so it spends through the budget without finding the buyers it should and the cost per purchase stays high.

educate.base:
An Advantage+ Sales Campaign (ASC) leans harder on the conversion signal than a manual campaign does because the same automation that picks the audience, the placements, and the creative is also learning from the purchases the dataset reports. If that signal is thin or unreliable, the automation learns from bad data and delivery suffers in ways that look like a creative or audience problem.

Three signal problems show up most. A purchase event firing only from the browser pixel, with no server-side Conversions API (CAPI) backup, loses conversions to browser restrictions and App Tracking Transparency (ATT), so the campaign sees fewer purchases than happened. The same purchase counted twice, once from the pixel and once from CAPI without a shared event ID for deduplication, inflates the count and teaches the model the wrong lesson. A dataset receiving too few purchases per week leaves the campaign below the volume automated delivery needs to stabilize.

The dataset's overview and the Events Manager diagnostics report each of these. They show whether CAPI is connected, whether browser and server events are being deduplicated, and the recent purchase event volume. Aggregated Event Measurement (AEM) also caps how many events a domain can optimize toward, which matters when the purchase event is competing with other events for a priority slot.

A few signs suggest the signal feeding the campaign deserves a closer look:
  • The purchase event fires from the pixel only, with no Conversions API connection on the dataset
  • Events Manager shows purchase events that are not being deduplicated across browser and server
  • Recent purchase volume on the dataset is low enough that delivery never leaves the learning phase
A campaign starved of good data cannot deliver well regardless of its creative or audience, so you may want to confirm the purchase signal is complete and clean before judging those inputs.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the purchase event volume is usually high enough to support automated delivery, so the signal problem is more often quality than quantity, for example a pixel-only purchase event with no Conversions API backup or browser and server purchases that are not deduplicated. You may want to confirm the confirmation page sends both a browser and a server purchase event with a shared event ID so the count is complete and not doubled.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, a true catalog sales campaign is rare, so an Advantage+ Sales Campaign here is usually optimizing toward a purchase-like or lead event with naturally lower volume. You may want to confirm the dataset receives enough of that event each week for delivery to leave the learning phase because a sparse signal hurts automated delivery more than a manual campaign.

investigate.intro: Confirm the purchase signal feeding the Advantage+ Sales Campaign is complete and clean by reading the dataset overview and the Events Manager diagnostics.

meta_events_manager.label: Meta Events Manager
steps: Open Events Manager and select the dataset the campaign optimizes toward. Open the purchase event and note whether it is received from the browser, the Conversions API, or both.
Check the deduplication status on the purchase event. Events arriving from both sources should report as deduplicated through a shared event ID rather than counted twice.

Read the recent purchase event volume and any diagnostics warnings, for example a missing Conversions API connection or events not matching, and note whether the weekly volume is high enough for delivery to leave the learning phase.

meta_ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: Open the Advantage+ Sales Campaign in Ads Manager and check whether its ad sets are stuck in the learning phase or show a 'Learning limited' status.
A campaign that cannot exit learning, paired with a thin or noisy purchase signal in Events Manager, points to a signal problem rather than a creative or audience one.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for the dataset the campaign optimizes toward, whether the Conversions API is connected, whether browser and server purchase events are deduplicated, and the recent weekly purchase volume relative to what delivery needs to stabilize. A campaign optimizing toward a pixel-only purchase event, an undeduplicated event, or a volume too low to exit the learning phase is a fail recorded with the dataset and the specific signal problem. A complete, deduplicated, sufficient signal passes.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Conversions API status, the deduplication status, and the recent purchase volume for the dataset above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Advantage+ Sales Campaign is optimizing toward a thin or unreliable purchase signal. Automated delivery learns from the conversions the dataset reports, and a signal that is pixel-only, undeduplicated, or too sparse teaches the model from incomplete or noisy data, so the campaign spends the budget without finding the buyers it should. {{voice_recommend}} connecting the Conversions API alongside the browser pixel, deduplicating the two through a shared event ID, and confirming the dataset receives enough weekly purchases for delivery to leave the learning phase. After the change, recheck the dataset in Events Manager for a complete, deduplicated signal and watch the campaign's cost per purchase.
